---
title: "DEEP: Future Fit"
author: "by Jess"
format:
    revealjs:
        theme: [solarized, custom.scss]
        slideNumber: true
        show-slide-number: all
        #reference-location: document
        #logo: images/logo.png
        footer: "https://deep-future-fit.netlify.app/"
        chalkboard:
            theme: whiteboard
        #code-annotations: hover
        css: styles.css
        mermaid:
          theme: default
        highlight-style: github
        #highlight-style: monochrome
        #logo: qr-code.png
        #multiplex: true
        code-block-height: 400px
editor:
    render-on-save: true

---

# Review

# Mobile Apps

## React Native
React Native is a framework for building *cross-platform* mobile apps.

A framework is a set of tools and libraries that make it easier to build software.

## Expo
Expo is also a framework, but its focus is on:

- Automating the build, test, and deployment process
- Sharing code between platforms (e.g. Android and iOS)
- Providing a simulation environment

## Getting Started

In a terminal:
```{.sh code-line-numbers="false"}
# Install Expo CLI globally
npm install -g expo-cli

# Create a new project
npx create-expo-app MyFirstApp

# Navigate to project folder
cd MyFirstApp

# Start the development server
npx expo start
```

Expo creates a React Native project for you

::: {.notes}
demo how to open on the simulator and on the phone
:::

## Components
Components are like lego pieces that you can combine to build a mobile app.

The [root]{.highlight-red} component is the `App` function by default.

Everything else starts from the [root]{.highlight-red} component.

::: {.notes}
go over the project folder
:::

## Creating Components {auto-animate="true"}

Create components using HTML and other components.

```{.jsx filename="App.js" code-line-numbers=1-7|9-17}
function Greeting({name}) { // name is a prop (aka parameter)
  return (
    <View>
      <Text>Hello, {name}!</Text> // Curly braces interpolate the value of `name` into the text
    </View>
  );
}

export default function App() {
  return (
    <View>
      <TouchableOpacity>
        <Text>Press Me</Text>
      </TouchableOpacity>
    </View>
  );
}
```

## Creating Components {auto-animate="true"}

We can then use this component in our `App` component.

```{.jsx filename="App.js" code-line-numbers=9-17}
function Greeting({name}) { // name is a prop (aka parameter)
  return (
    <View>
      <Text>Hello, {name}!</Text> // Curly braces interpolate the value of `name` into the text
    </View>
  );
}

export default function App() {
  return (
    <View>
      <Greeting name="Sarah" />
      <Greeting name="Mike" />
      <TouchableOpacity>
        <Text>Press Me</Text>
      </TouchableOpacity>
    </View>
  );
}
```

## Built-In Components

React Native provides a set of built-in components that you are encouraged to use.

- View
- Text
- TouchableOpacity
- Image
- ScrollView
- ... and more

## Styling Components {auto-animate="true"}

Create a `StyleSheet` object to define CSS-like styles.

```{.jsx filename="App.js" code-line-numbers=19-50|23-28|1-17}
function Greeting({name}) { // name is a prop (aka parameter)
  return (
    <View>
      <Text>Hello, {name}!</Text> // Curly braces interpolate the value of `name` into the text
    </View>
  );
}

export default function App() {
  return (
    <View>
      <Greeting name="Sarah" />
      <Greeting name="Mike" />
      <TouchableOpacity>
        <Text>Press Me</Text>
      </TouchableOpacity>
    </View>
  );
}

const styles = StyleSheet.create({
  // This is CSS-like styling
  container: { // Class name
    flex: 1,
    justifyContent: 'center',
    alignItems: 'center',
    backgroundColor: '#fff',
  },
  greetingContainer: {
    marginVertical: 10,
    padding: 15,
    backgroundColor: '#e0e0e0',
    borderRadius: 8,
  },
  greetingText: {
    fontSize: 18,
    color: '#333',
  },
  button: {
    marginTop: 20,
    backgroundColor: '#007AFF',
    padding: 12,
    borderRadius: 8,
  },
  buttonText: {
    color: 'white',
    fontSize: 16,
    fontWeight: 'bold',
  },
});
```

## Styling Components {auto-animate="true"}

Apply the styles using the `style` prop.

```{.jsx filename="App.js" code-line-numbers=1-17}
function Greeting({name}) { // name is a prop (aka parameter)
  return (
    <View style={styles.greetingContainer}>
      <Text style={styles.greetingText}>Hello, {name}!</Text> // Curly braces interpolate the value of `name` into the text
    </View>
  );
}

export default function App() {
  return (
    <View style={styles.container}>
      <Greeting name="Sarah" />
      <Greeting name="Mike" />
      <TouchableOpacity style={styles.button}>
        <Text style={styles.buttonText}>Press Me</Text>
      </TouchableOpacity>
    </View>
  );
}

const styles = StyleSheet.create({
  // This is CSS-like styling
  container: { // Class name
    flex: 1,
    justifyContent: 'center',
    alignItems: 'center',
    backgroundColor: '#fff',
  },
  greetingContainer: {
    marginVertical: 10,
    padding: 15,
    backgroundColor: '#e0e0e0',
    borderRadius: 8,
  },
  greetingText: {
    fontSize: 18,
    color: '#333',
  },
  button: {
    marginTop: 20,
    backgroundColor: '#007AFF',
    padding: 12,
    borderRadius: 8,
  },
  buttonText: {
    color: 'white',
    fontSize: 16,
    fontWeight: 'bold',
  },
});
```

# Dynamic

::: {.notes}
that's all we need for a static app
:::

## State

State lets a component “remember” information like user input (i.e. a variable for a component)

```{.jsx filename="CounterApp.js" code-line-numbers=|6-7|11|16}
import React, { useState } from 'react';
import { View, Text, TextInput, TouchableOpacity, StyleSheet } from 'react-native';

const CounterApp = () => {
  // useState returns [currentValue, setterFunction]
  const [count, setCount] = useState(0);
  const [name, setName] = useState('');

  return (
    <View style={styles.container}>
      <Text style={styles.title}>Counter: {count}</Text>

      <View style={styles.buttonRow}>
        <TouchableOpacity
          style={styles.button}
          onPress={() => setCount(count + 1)}
        >
          <Text style={styles.buttonText}>Increment</Text>
        </TouchableOpacity>

        <TouchableOpacity
          style={[styles.button, styles.resetButton]}
          onPress={() => setCount(0)}
        >
          <Text style={styles.buttonText}>Reset</Text>
        </TouchableOpacity>
      </View>

      <TextInput
        style={styles.input}
        placeholder="Enter your name"
        value={name}
        onChangeText={setName}
      />

      {name ? <Text>Hello, {name}!</Text> : null}
    </View>
  );
};

const styles = StyleSheet.create({
  container: { flex: 1, justifyContent: 'center', alignItems: 'center', padding: 20 },
  title: { fontSize: 24, fontWeight: 'bold', marginBottom: 20 },
  buttonRow: { flexDirection: 'row', marginBottom: 20 },
  button: { backgroundColor: '#007AFF', padding: 12, borderRadius: 8, marginHorizontal: 5 },
  resetButton: { backgroundColor: '#FF3B30' },
  buttonText: { color: 'white', fontSize: 16 },
  input: { borderWidth: 1, borderColor: '#ccc', padding: 10, borderRadius: 8, width: 200, marginBottom: 20 }
});

export default CounterApp;
```

## Effects

Logic inside components:

- Rendering code
    - "pure" calculations, predictable results
- Event handlers
    - functions that *do* things, unpredictable, triggered by events

::: {.fragment .fade-in}
- Effects
    - a side effect caused by rendering
:::

## Effects

React resolves effects after rendering the page or a specific dependency.

```{.jsx filename="DataFetcher.js" code-line-numbers=|5-7|35-40|9-33|35|42|48-52}
import React, { useState, useEffect } from 'react';
import { View, Text, ActivityIndicator, StyleSheet } from 'react-native';

const DataFetcher = () => {
  const [data, setData] = useState(null);
  const [loading, setLoading] = useState(true);
  const [error, setError] = useState(null);

  useEffect(() => {
    // This runs after component mounts
    console.log('Component mounted - fetching data');

    const fetchData = async () => {
      try {
        // Simulating API call
        const response = await new Promise((resolve) =>
          setTimeout(() => resolve({ message: 'Hello from API!' }), 2000)
        );
        setData(response);
      } catch (err) {
        setError(err.message);
      } finally {
        setLoading(false);
      }
    };

    fetchData();

    // Cleanup function (runs before unmount or before next effect)
    return () => {
      console.log('Cleanup - component unmounting or dependencies changed');
    };
  }, []); // Empty array = runs only once on mount, add dependencies here if needed

  if (loading) return (
    <View style={styles.center}>
      <ActivityIndicator size="large" color="#007AFF" />
      <Text>Loading...</Text>
    </View>
  );

  if (error) return (
    <View style={styles.center}>
      <Text style={styles.error}>Error: {error}</Text>
    </View>
  );

  return (
    <View style={styles.center}>
      <Text>{data?.message}</Text>
    </View>
  );
};

const styles = StyleSheet.create({
  center: { flex: 1, justifyContent: 'center', alignItems: 'center' },
  error: { color: 'red' }
});

export default DataFetcher;
```

::: {.notes}
walk through the componentn and ask them what kind of logic each part is
:::

# Tutorial

# Why is it Lossy?

## Inteference
BLE operates on the 2.4 GHz frequency band, which is shared with other wireless technologies.

- ISM devices (e.g. microwave ovens, medical diathermy devices)
- WiFi
- Bluetooth

## Channel Hopping
A channel is a specific frequency channel in between 2.4 GHz and 2.5 GHz.

A BLE device constantly hops between channels to avoid interference.

TODO: include diagram

## Packet Loss
Packet loss may occur whenever there is interference.

The BLE device changes channels but any lossed packets need to be resent.
